content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 269 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_001_page_001.jpg

1. Symeon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ, to those who have obtained a faith of equal standing with ours by the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ:

2. May grace and peace be multiplied to you in the knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord.

3. As his divine power has granted to us all things pertaining to life and godliness, through the knowledge of him who called us by his own glory and virtue,

4. through which he has granted to us the precious and exceedingly great promises, so that through these you might become partakers of the divine nature, having escaped from the corruption that is in the world through desire.

5. And for this very reason, bringing all diligence, supply in your faith virtue, and in your virtue knowledge,

6. and in your knowledge self-control, and in your self-control endurance, and in your endurance godliness,

7. and in your godliness brotherly affection, and in your brotherly affection love.

8. For if these things are yours and are increasing, they render you neither idle nor unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.

9. For the one in whom these things are not present is blind, being shortsighted, having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of his former sins.

10. Therefore, brothers, be all the more diligent to make your calling and election firm; for if you do these things, you will never stumble.

11. For in this way the entrance into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ will be richly supplied to you.

12. Therefore I shall always be ready to remind you concerning these things, though you know them and have been established in the truth that is present with you.

13. And I consider it right, as long as I am in this tent, to stir you up by way of reminder,

14. knowing that the putting off of my tent is soon, even as our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me.

15. And I will also be diligent that at any time after my departure you may be able to call these things to remembrance.

16. For we did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of that one's majesty.

17. For having received from God the Father honor and glory, when such a voice was borne to him by the Majestic Glory: "This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I am well pleased" —

18. and this voice we ourselves heard borne from heaven, being with him on the holy mountain.

19. And we have the prophetic word made more sure, to which you do well to pay attention, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts,

20. knowing this first of all, that no prophecy of Scripture comes about by one's own interpretation.

21. For no prophecy was ever brought forth by the will of man, but men spoke from God, being carried along by the Holy Spirit.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Symeon Peter" (v.1):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΜΕΩΝ (Symeon), the Hebraic form of the name, rather than the Hellenized Σιμων (Simon) found in later manuscripts. This archaic spelling is shared with Acts 15:14 and is considered by many scholars to be the more difficult — and therefore more original — reading. A formal translation preserves this distinctive orthography.

2. **"Our God and Savior Jesus Christ" (v.1):** The Greek τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν καὶ σωτῆρος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ follows the Granville Sharp rule: a single article governing two nouns joined by καί, where the first noun is not a proper name, identifies them as the same referent. This is one of the clearest christological affirmations of Christ's deity in the New Testament.

3. **"Partakers of the divine nature" (v.4):** θείας κοινωνοὶ φύσεως — a phrase with both Hellenistic philosophical resonance (participation in the divine) and Jewish covenantal background. Peter does not teach ontological absorption into God but rather moral transformation through the promises. The escape from "corruption through desire" (φθορᾶς τῆς ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἐν ἐπιθυμίᾳ) frames this participation in ethical terms.

4. **The virtue chain (vv.5-7):** The literary form is a sorites (chain syllogism), common in Stoic moral philosophy (cf. Romans 5:3-5). Each virtue builds upon the previous, culminating in ἀγάπη (love). The structure moves from the theological foundation (faith) through moral discipline (virtue, knowledge, self-control, endurance) to relational qualities (godliness, brotherly affection, love). Peter Christianizes a Hellenistic rhetorical form.

5. **"Being shortsighted" (v.9):** μυωπάζων — literally "closing the eyes, squinting." Sinaiticus preserves this rare word. Combined with τυφλός (blind), it creates an apparent contradiction: how can one be both blind and shortsighted? The sense is willful spiritual blindness — the person who can see near things (worldly concerns) but has lost sight of the distant reality (eschatological hope and past cleansing).

6. **"The putting off of my tent" (v.14):** ἡ ἀπόθεσις τοῦ σκηνώματός μου — "tent" (σκήνωμα) as a metaphor for the body echoes 2 Corinthians 5:1-4 and draws on the ancient Jewish association of the tabernacle/tent with transient earthly dwelling. The word ταχινή ("soon, swift") indicates Peter writes in awareness of impending death, traditionally connected to Jesus' prophecy in John 21:18-19.

7. **"Eyewitnesses of that one's majesty" (v.16):** ἐπόπται — a technical term from the Greek mystery religions for the highest grade of initiate who has witnessed the sacred rites. Peter repurposes this vocabulary: the apostles' "initiation" was not into a secret cult but into the public, historical Transfiguration event. The formal register of the word underscores the gravity of the testimony.

8. **The Transfiguration account (vv.17-18):** Peter's version of the heavenly voice combines elements from the Synoptic accounts. "My Son, my Beloved" (ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός μου) echoes the baptismal and Transfiguration voices (Matt 17:5; Mark 9:7; Luke 9:35). Sinaiticus reads with the possessive μου after ἀγαπητός, emphasizing the intimate divine relationship. "The holy mountain" (τῷ ἁγίῳ ὄρει) is unnamed — its holiness derives not from geography but from theophany.

9. **"The prophetic word made more sure" (v.19):** βεβαιότερον — the comparative adjective can be read two ways: (a) the Transfiguration experience confirms what the prophets already said, or (b) the prophetic word is inherently more certain than even eyewitness experience. Reading (a) fits the flow of the argument better: apostolic experience validates prophetic testimony, forming a double witness. The "morning star" (φωσφόρος, literally "light-bearer") is Christ himself (cf. Rev 22:16).

10. **"By one's own interpretation" (v.20):** ἰδίας ἐπιλύσεως — ἐπίλυσις means "unloosing, explanation." The point is not that individuals cannot interpret Scripture, but that no prophecy originated from the prophet's own analysis of events. Verse 21 clarifies: prophets spoke as they were "carried along" (φερόμενοι) by the Holy Spirit. The verb φέρω here suggests being borne along like a ship by the wind — the Spirit is the driving force behind prophetic utterance.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
